title: Coal Gasification and Coal Controller's Organization (CCO)
focus: CCO's mandate, Syngas composition, and the Coal Gasification Financial Incentive Scheme (CGFIS).
rationale: A distinct concept cluster covering coal administration and gasification technology/schemes before the shift to energy efficiency.
sectionExcerpt: CCO collects coal production data and acts as the appellate authority for disputes over coal grade and size. Coal Gasification converts coal into syngas (CO, H2, CO2, CH4). The Coal Gasification Financial Incentive Scheme (CGFIS) launched in 2024 aims to achieve 100 million tonnes of coal gasification by 2030.
evidenceBullets: CCO acts as the appellate authority for disputes between consumers and owners regarding the declaration of coal grade and size.
Syngas is a mixture of carbon monoxide (CO), hydrogen (H2), carbon dioxide (CO2), methane (CH4), and other gases.
The Coal Gasification Financial Incentive Scheme (CGFIS) aims to achieve 100 million tonnes of coal gasification by 2030.

title: ADEETIE Scheme
focus: Objectives, targets, and financial components of the ADEETIE scheme for MSMEs.
rationale: A standalone Ministry of Power scheme targeting energy efficiency specifically in MSMEs.
sectionExcerpt: Assistance in Deploying Energy Efficient Technologies in Industries & Establishments (ADEETIE) helps MSMEs reduce energy consumption by 30-50%. Implemented by BEE under the Ministry of Power for 3 years (FY26-FY28) with a ₹1000 crore outlay. It offers 5% interest subvention for Micro/Small and 3% for Medium enterprises.
evidenceBullets: ADEETIE is implemented by the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) under the Ministry of Power.
It targets MSMEs with a Udyam ID to reduce energy consumption by 30-50%.
The scheme provides a 5% interest subvention for Micro and Small Enterprises and 3% for Medium Enterprises.
The budgetary outlay is ₹1000 crore over a 3-year duration (FY 2025-26 to FY 2027-28).

title: CAFE Norms and Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)
focus: Applicability and timelines of CAFE norms, and BEE's statutory framework and reports.
rationale: Groups the Corporate Average Fuel Efficiency norms with the parent regulatory body (BEE) and its related indices.
sectionExcerpt: CAFE-3 norms will be in force from April 2027 to March 2032, applicable to passenger cars up to 9 seats and 3,500 kg. BEE operates under the Energy Conservation Act 2001. BEE, along with the Alliance for an Energy Efficient Economy, released the State Energy Efficiency Index (SEEI) 2024.
evidenceBullets: CAFE-3 norms will come into force from 1st April 2027 until 31st March 2032.
CAFE norms apply to passenger cars with a seating capacity not exceeding 9 persons and a gross vehicle weight not exceeding 3,500 kg.
BEE was established under the legislative framework of the Energy Conservation Act 2001.
The State Energy Efficiency Index (SEEI) 2024 was developed by BEE in collaboration with the Alliance for an Energy Efficient Economy.

title: Bio-Input Resource Centres (BRCs) and National Mission on Natural Farming (NMNF)
focus: Guidelines for BRCs and the structural/financial details of the NMNF.
rationale: BRCs are a direct component of the NMNF; they share the same ministry and overarching goals.
sectionExcerpt: The Ministry of Agriculture released guidelines for 10,000 BRCs under NMNF, providing Rs 1 lakh financial assistance per centre. NMNF is a Centrally Sponsored Scheme (90:10 for Hilly/NE, 60:40 for others) running till 2025-26, aiming to reach 1 crore farmers and establish 15,000 clusters. 30,000 Krishi Sakhis are deployed to scale up practices.
evidenceBullets: BRCs provide Rs 1 lakh financial assistance for setting up cluster-level enterprises to supply biological inputs.
NMNF is a Centrally Sponsored Scheme with a 90:10 funding ratio for Hilly & North Eastern States and 60:40 for all other States.
NMNF aims to reach 1 crore farmers and initiate Natural Farming in a 7.5 lakh Ha area by 2025-26.
30,000 Krishi Sakhis (Community Resource Persons) are utilized for the easy scaling up of Natural Farming practices.

title: Natural Farming Practices and Comparison with Organic Farming
focus: Core components of natural farming (Beejamrit, Jivamrit, Whapasa) and its distinctions from organic farming.
rationale: Focuses purely on the agronomic concepts, techniques, and comparative analysis of farming systems.
sectionExcerpt: Natural Farming is a chemical-free, low-input system relying on on-farm inputs like Desi Cow products. Components include Beejamrit (seed treatment), Jivamrit (bio-stimulant), Mulching, and Whapasa (activating earthworms). Unlike Organic Farming, Natural Farming uses no external inputs, avoids compost/minerals for soil correction, and relies on surface decomposition rather than plowing.
evidenceBullets: Beejamrit involves the treatment of seeds using cow dung, urine, and lime-based formulations.
Whapasa refers to activating earthworms in the soil for water vapour condensation.
Organic Farming involves off-farm purchased inputs, whereas Natural Farming relies strictly on on-farm inputs based on the Desi Cow.
Natural Farming does not allow the use of compost, vermicompost, or mined minerals for soil correction, unlike Organic Farming.

title: Globally Important Agricultural Heritage Systems (GIAHS)
focus: FAO's GIAHS programme and newly added sites.
rationale: A distinct international agricultural initiative mentioned at the end of the packet.
sectionExcerpt: Six new sites were added to the FAO's GIAHS Programme, including a traditional agroforestry system for the erva-mate plant in Brazil, specialised farming systems in China (pearl mussels, white tea, pears), and an ancestral system preserving vital food crops in Mexico.
evidenceBullets: GIAHS is a programme operated by the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO).
Newly added GIAHS sites include an erva-mate agroforestry system in Brazil and pearl mussel farming in China.
